YU-SC-30-E-01  ·  ro-yuan-023

Yuan Stoneware Vase with Islamic Scrollwork

元代白地褐彩阿拉伯卷草纹瓶 — Yuándài Báidì Hécǎi Ālābó Juǎncǎo Wén Píng

Cross-Cultural · Islamic Scrollwork Yuan Dynasty · 1279–1368 CE Zhejiang–Jingdezhen Tradition Iron-Brown Underglaze · White Slip Height: 30 cm Dronova Method Applied

Object Description

This stoneware vase represents a significant category of Yuan dynasty ceramic production in which Chinese technological traditions meet the international artistic vocabulary disseminated across Eurasia by the Mongol Empire. The vessel combines the pear-shaped bottle profile of southern Chinese high-fired stoneware — globular lower body, tall cylindrical neck, narrow shoulder, flat mouth, low foot ring — with an all-over decorative programme derived from Islamic arabesque ornament: continuous spiralling vine scrolls, palmette terminals, circular floral medallions, and leaf fillers organised in a rhythmic but non-repeating pattern covering the entire exterior from foot to mouth rim.

The technical construction follows the standard southern production sequence: a light buff stoneware body — coarse-grained with quartz inclusions and dense firing texture — receives an even white slip ground, over which the scrollwork is freely painted in iron-rich brown and black pigment before a single application of transparent glaze with a faint celadon tint. A double iron-brown line borders the neck junction, echoing the compositional conventions of contemporaneous Cizhou-tradition wares while adapting them to the Islamic decorative programme.

The decorative vocabulary — spiralling vines, palmette terminals, circular vegetal rhythms — belongs to the international ornamental language shared by Persian ceramics, Ilkhanid manuscripts, Islamic metalwork, and Central Asian textiles of the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries. Its appearance on a Chinese stoneware body of this scale and technical quality represents artistic exchange along the Silk Road under Mongol patronage, placing this vessel within the broader context of the fourteenth-century cross-cultural ceramic production documented in the collections of the Victoria and Albert Museum, Freer Gallery of Art, and Ashmolean Museum.

Cross-cultural significance: The arabesque ornament on this vessel is not a superficial borrowing but a fully integrated decorative programme executed with the same technical confidence as Chinese floral scrollwork of the same period. The rhythmic, non-geometrically-repeating organisation of the motifs — spiralling vines generated from a continuous stem rather than stamped or moulded — indicates freehand workshop painting by an artisan familiar with the full visual grammar of the Islamic arabesque tradition.

Microscopic Analysis (GOM)

Оптическое исследование проводилось по Методу Дроновой при увеличениях ×10, ×20, ×40 в режимах отражённого и косого освещения. Приоритетные зоны: поверхность глазури в зоне декора и граница пигмент/ангоб.

Zone A — Поверхность глазури, пузырьки, пигмент

GOM · ×20
×20

Однородная стеклоподобная матрица прозрачной глазури с рассеянными газовыми пузырьками неравномерного размера. Тёмная зона — железный пигмент с диффузным проникновением в ангоб. Белая зона — чистый ангоб с мелкозернистой текстурой. Признаков повторного нанесения глазури нет.

При ×20 поверхность глазури демонстрирует однородную стеклоподобную матрицу с рассеянными газовыми пузырьками, характерными для высокотемпературного одиночного обжига. На границе железного пигмента и белого ангоба отчётливо видно диффузное проникновение красителя в ангоб — признак нанесения пигмента по ещё влажному ангобу. В зоне высокой концентрации пигмента наблюдается тёмно-коричневый почти-чёрный оттенок, аналогичный эффекту пилинг-энд-хипинг в кобальтовых юаньских вещах, но обусловленный концентрацией железа, а не кобальта.

Zone B — Кистевая работа, переменный нажим

Макро · ×10
×10

Переменный нажим кисти: тёмные (почти чёрные) зоны скопления пигмента и охристо-коричневые линии при меньшей загрузке. Сужающиеся концы, слияния и естественная асимметрия завитков. Непрерывное движение руки без остановок — признак опытного мастера.

Анализ кистевой работы при ×10 выявляет все признаки аутентичного ручного исполнения: переменный нажим, создающий тональный диапазон от почти чёрного до охристо-коричневого; сужающиеся концы линий, характерные для кистевой каллиграфии; естественное слияние завитков в точках пересечения; отсутствие следов механического повторения или штамповки. Ритмичная, но не идентично повторяющаяся организация мотивов подтверждает фриручный исполнение в мастерской.

Diagnostic Interpretation

Объект оценивался по девятикритериальной диагностической матрице Метода Дроновой для юаньских каменных масс с подглазурной росписью. Все критерии удовлетворены.

Diagnostic Category Observed Assessment
MorphologyPear-shaped Yuan bottle: globular body, tall cylindrical neck, flat mouth, low foot ring✓ Yuan stoneware form
BodyLight buff high-fired stoneware; coarse quartz inclusions; dense compact texture; no modern industrial porosity✓ Southern Yuan production
White slipEven ground application; natural ageing; complete surface coverage; no secondary slip layer✓ Authentic slip ground
Iron pigmentVariable iron-brown to black tones; diffuse penetration into slip; tonal gradient at brush load variation✓ Authentic underglaze iron
BrushworkFreehand painting; varying pressure; tapering line endings; no mechanical repetition; natural asymmetry✓ Workshop hand-painting
GlazeTransparent; faint celadon tint; natural thickness variation; gas bubbles; single firing✓ High-temperature single firing
Foot ringUnglazed; wheel throwing marks; light buff coarse body; porous surface; no artificial colouring✓ Authentic kiln manufacture
IconographyArabesque scrollwork: spiralling vines, palmette terminals, circular medallions — fully integrated programme✓ Mongol period cross-cultural
Museum comparandaV&A, Freer Gallery, Ashmolean: comparable bottle form, white slip, iron-brown painting, Islamic ornament✓ Strong Positive

Diagnostic Checklist

  • Pear-shaped Yuan bottle profile with tall cylindrical neck — canonical southern Chinese high-fired stoneware form of the Yuan period
  • Light buff coarse stoneware body with quartz inclusions — consistent with Zhejiang–Jingdezhen production tradition
  • Even white slip ground with natural ageing — authentic single-application slip, no secondary overpainting detected
  • Iron-brown to black underglaze pigment with diffuse slip penetration — confirms wet-on-wet application, not post-firing addition
  • Tonal gradient in iron pigment zones: dark black at maximum concentration, ochre-brown at thinner passages — iron-concentration dependent firing response
  • Freehand brushwork with varying pressure and tapering line endings — workshop hand-painting confirmed; no mechanical reproduction indicators
  • Transparent glaze with faint celadon tint and gas bubbles in matrix — high-temperature single reduction firing
  • Unglazed foot ring with wheel throwing marks and coarse buff body — authentic kiln manufacture, no artificial colouring
  • Arabesque scrollwork programme fully integrated: freehand, rhythmically organised, non-geometrically repeating — consistent with Mongol-period cross-cultural workshop production
  • No evidence of modern restoration, re-slip, reglaze, overpaint, or artificial ageing

Diagnostic Fingerprint

Authentication at a glance — полный диагностический портрет объекта.

Diagnostic MarkerAuthentic Yuan Example
BodyBuff stoneware, coarse quartz
SlipWhite, even, single application
PigmentIron-brown/black, diffuse
BrushworkFreehand, variable pressure
Diagnostic MarkerAuthentic Yuan Example
GlazeTransparent, celadon tint
FootUnglazed, wheel marks, buff
OrnamentArabic arabesque, integrated
ComparandaV&A, Freer, Ashmolean
Сочетание диффузного проникновения железного пигмента в ангоб, фриручной кистевой работы с переменным нажимом и полностью интегрированной арабескной программы образует диагностическую триаду, специфичную для юаньских межкультурных мастерских Шёлкового пути. Все три критерия удовлетворяются в данном образце.

Cross-References

ro-yuan-018 — Yuan Cizhou Headrest with Lion and Peony

Iron-brown-on-white-slip technique comparison: both objects use iron-rich mineral pigment on white slip ground. The Cizhou headrest employs sgraffito in addition to painting; this vase relies on brushwork alone. The diffuse pigment-slip penetration mechanism is identical in both cases.

→ View ro-yuan-018

ro-yuan-022 — Yuan Blue-and-White Lobed Dish with Double Geese

Underglaze metal-oxide painting comparison: iron-brown (ro-yuan-023) vs imported cobalt blue (ro-yuan-022). Both exploit the concentration-dependent tonal response of metal oxides in a reducing kiln atmosphere; piling-and-heaping in cobalt has a direct parallel in the dark-zone pooling of iron pigment on this vase.

→ View ro-yuan-022

ro-yuan-017 — Yuan Black-Glazed Meiping with Dragon

Iron oxide decorative programme across Yuan traditions: reserve-incised iron-black ground (ro-yuan-017) vs iron-brown freehand painting on white slip (ro-yuan-023). Both exploit iron oxide as the primary chromatic agent; forming method and kiln atmosphere differ, producing contrasting visual effects from the same material basis.

→ View ro-yuan-017
